summary: VitaDAO funding proposal (VDP-163) for developing antisense oligonucleotide (AON) therapeutics targeting FOXO3 isoforms to treat degenerative disc disease (DDD). The proposal outlines a two-phase preclinical program with a go/no-go milestone. Phase 1 (covered by this funding request) includes AON design, validation in 2D cell culture, and testing in ex vivo organ-on-chip models. Budget: £219,846 (~$298k). IP ownership goes to VitaDAO. PI: Professor Lorna Harries (University of Exeter).

content_blocks.title: Proposal Header and Revision Notes
content_blocks.content: VDP-163 is a revised version of VDP-146 (approved on Discourse but failed quorum). Key revisions: prioritizes go-to-market indication (DDD) from outset; includes intermediate go/no-go milestone capping initial risk at half budget; gives IP ownership to VitaDAO (not licensing); plans IPT raise with $50k anchor from VitaDAO; includes SpineDAO expression of interest; increases budget by $50k; removes C. elegans testing. Project team: PI Lorna Harries, PhD; VitaDAO lead TBD. Longevity dealflow team: Shepherd Paolo Binetti; Squad members Rhys Anderson, Daniel Hussey, Matt McAlister; Sourced by Adrian Matysek.

content_blocks.title: Simple Summary and Problem Statement
content_blocks.content: Therapeutic approach: Design modified RNA molecules (antisense oligonucleotides/AONs) that selectively switch FOXO3's 'good' isoform (long form) on in human cells and mini-disc models. Target indication: Chronic lower back pain from degenerative disc disease (DDD/IDD). FOXO3 background: One of four human FOXO genes; evolutionarily conserved central network orchestrating autophagy, mitochondrial quality control, DNA-damage repair, metabolic flexibility, inflammatory restraint. One FOXO3 haplotype (rs13217795-rs4946932-rs9400239) associated with ~36% increased chance of exceptional longevity across >11 human populations. FOXO3 has never been successfully pharmacologically targeted. DDD epidemiology: Leading cause of physical disability; 12-16% of adults visit GP annually; UK: 3.5M annual incident cases, 17.3M prevalent cases, 3.1M suffering year-round, 116M production days lost. Current treatment: Steroids (adverse effects) or surgery (recovery time, infection risk, anaesthesia mortality, not durable, can worsen disease). No disease-modifying options exist.

content_blocks.title: Solution: FOXO3 Isoform Modulation via Oligonucleotides
content_blocks.content: Discovery: Association between extreme lifespan and FOXO3 genetic variation likely mediated by alternatively expressed mRNA isoforms. FOXO3 expresses three linear mRNA isoforms and one circular RNA. FOXO3-long: full-length, encodes complete fork-head DNA-binding domain, ubiquitous. FOXO3-short: lacks key regulatory motifs, enriched in aging muscle and degenerating tissues. Aging and metabolic stress progressively tilt balance toward short form, diminishing stress response capacity. Modality: Oligonucleotide drugs for regulated control of FOXO3 isoform expression. Can enhance expression with small activating RNAs (saRNAs) or steric hindrance of negative regulatory elements; can repress via RNAi or steric blockage. Steric hindrance ASOs already proven safe/effective in clinic (SMA, DMD). PI expertise: ~30 years RNA biology; knowhow in oligo design/delivery as founder/CSO of SENISCA Ltd. Safety profile: Extremely safe vs small molecule; oligo drugs act on specific 20-25 nucleotide region, bind nowhere else if correctly designed. Fastest growing drug modality (4 new approvals in 2023). Delivery: Local (naked in saline) or using lipid nanoparticles, peptides, antibody conjugates, hydrogel carriers. Allow drugging of 'undruggable' targets like transcription factors.

content_blocks.title: FOXO3 in DDD: Disease-Modifying Approach
content_blocks.content: Evidence linking FOXO3 to DDD: FOXO3 expression strongly correlated with DDD in mouse and human (ref 18). Associated with extracellular matrix degradation and nucleus pulposus cell death in vitro and in vivo (ref 19). FOXO3 overexpression ameliorates intervertebral disc degeneration by decreasing NLRP3-mediated pyroptosis (ref 20). Balance of FOXO3 isoforms important in bone remodeling; absence leads to elevated osteoclasts (bone resorption); short isoforms suppress bone remodeling (ref 21). Delivery route: Direct injection to intervertebral space (standard route). Duration: Oligonucleotide therapeutics have long duration of action, up to 6 months in some cases. Naked delivery: PI has proven track record delivering oligonucleotides to human primary cell models by gymnosis (via SENISCA role). Efficacy could be enhanced with DDD-specific formulation for later growth potential.

content_blocks.title: Go-to-Market Strategy: DDD as Lead Indication
content_blocks.content: Rationale for DDD as first indication: Clear causal link to FOXO3 in published research; local delivery via familiar route; less heterogeneous patient population than cancer/diabetes/CVD; large/growing market ($35B in 2025, projected $45B by 2029, 7.5% CAGR); less crowded competitive space than cancer/CVD/diabetes. Competitive landscape: Pipeline drugs are non-disease modifying (hydrogels), involve non-specific targeting of major pathways (TGFβ with tolerability/off-target risks), or biologicals (stem cells with limited availability, high cost, rejection issues). Competitive advantage: Targeted AON has very precise MoA, very limited off-target profile, improved safety especially with local administration due to limited systemic exposure. Injectable disease-modifying RNA therapy would compete favorably vs implants, stem-cell products, non-specific growth-factor blockers.

content_blocks.title: Future Label Expansion Opportunities
content_blocks.content: Table compares disease areas for FOXO3-targeted therapeutics beyond DDD. Columns: Disease area, Key advantages, Key challenges, Est. 2025 US market size, FOXO3 reference. Rows: (1) DDD/IDD: $35B market, local intradiscal injection avoids systemic PK, moderately crowded pipeline (~15 active assets vs >100 in CVD), disc height & pain endpoints validated; challenges: chronic low-back-pain space still competitive. (2) Cardiovascular disease: $160B market, huge market with hard biomarkers (LDL-C, BP, troponin), clear FDA pathways; challenges: highly crowded (>100 late-phase drugs), requires systemic delivery, lifestyle confounders. (3) Neurodegenerative: $20B market, orphan sub-indications give regulatory incentives, enormous unmet need; challenges: BBB delivery, high trial-failure rate, heterogeneous patients. (4) Cancer: $200B market, largest therapeutic market, fast-track/breakthrough pathways, biomarker niches allow premium pricing; challenges: extremely crowded, heterogeneous patients, complex safety expectations. (5) Autoimmune/immune dysfunction: US $170B market, chronic high-value biologic segment, clear clinical scales, potential for local dosing; challenges: Humira-class biologics dominate, heterogeneous patients, long systemic exposure, infection risk. (6) Sarcopenia/frailty: $4B projected, no FDA-approved drugs (first-in-class potential), aging biology directly relevant; challenges: regulatory endpoints debated (SPPB, gait speed), smaller spend, requires systemic delivery. (7) Diabetes & complications: $40B market, fast-growing, hard biomarkers (HbA1c, CGM), short PoC trials, clear reimbursement; challenges: very crowded (GLP-1, SGLT2, Tirzepatide boom), chronic systemic dosing, lifestyle/obesity confounders.

content_blocks.title: Relevance to Longevity and IP Roadmap
content_blocks.content: Longevity relevance: By re-balancing FOXO3 long/short isoforms, targets central node orchestrating autophagy, mitochondrial maintenance, DNA-damage repair, anti-inflammatory signaling—processes that slow functional decline of multiple tissues. DDD focus offers clear clinical path, but same antisense strategy can be retargeted systemically or to other organs for sarcopenia, vascular aging, immune dysfunction, metabolic disease. Modulates evolutionarily conserved hub rather than single downstream pathology, thus holds potential to extend healthspan (period free from chronic disability) and lifespan. IP strategy: Phase 1 output would be prototype oligonucleotides that modulate FOXO3 isoform usage/activity, plus demonstration of attenuation of senescence/disease markers in 2D and 3D tissue culture systems. This provides enough data for initial patent filing. Following initial filing, claims would be bolstered by in vivo data within 12 months (contingent on further funding).

content_blocks.title: Experimental Plan - Phase 1: Milestone 1a (AON Design)
content_blocks.content: Hypothesis: Targeted moderation of FOXO3 isoform expression profiles will attenuate cellular aging and tissue health parameters in vitro and ex vivo, and bring functional benefits in vivo. Milestone 1a Objective: Design and validate antisense oligonucleotides (AONs) that effectively and specifically modulate FOXO3 isoform expression in human monolayer culture of primary fibroblasts (NHDF), Human Nucleus Pulposus Cells (HNPC), and Human Annulus Fibrosus Cells (HAFC). Workplan: Design AONs to attenuate FOXO3 isoform usage via steric hindrance translation blockers against isoform-specific translation start sites of FOXO3-short or -long, or by blocking negative regulatory elements specific to each isoform. Standard technologies routinely used in Harries academic team and SENISCA industrial team (several oligonucleotides using similar MoA already developed). Efficiency of isoform switching measured by Western blot (band size difference and intensities using anti-FOXO3 antibody binding epitope present in both isoforms) and RT-qPCR. Possible to produce cells expressing primarily FOXO3-long, primarily FOXO3-short, or both. Outcome: Identification of at least two lead AON candidates demonstrating potent and specific modulation of FOXO3 isoforms and reduction in senescence markers. Provides foundational evidence of correct molecular outcome.

content_blocks.title: Experimental Plan - Phase 1: Milestone 1b (2D Efficacy)
content_blocks.content: Milestone 1b Objective: Determine if changes in FOXO3 isoform expression translate to functional rescue of senescent phenotype in cells relevant to DDD. Assess if AONs alter: (1) FOXO3 activity and subcellular localization; (2) functional rescue of DDD phenotypes including autophagy, mitochondrial function, senescent cell phenotype/accumulation rate. Workplan: Subcellular localization of FOXO3-long and FOXO3-short proteins assessed in NHDF, HNPC, HAFC monolayer by immunofluorescence microscopy. Cells induced to degenerative state resembling DDD using TNFα and IL-1β under serum-limiting conditions (ref 27). Changes in relative ability of FOXO3 isoforms to transactivate commercially available luciferase reporter construct containing FOXO binding sites assessed as measure of FOXO3 activity. Mitochondrial function: live cell measurement of mitochondrial respiration and glycolysis on Seahorse XF24 platform (refs 20,21) following oligonucleotide manipulation. Impact of isoform usage on senescence rate: senescence-associated beta galactosidase (SA-beta-Gal) activity, EdU incorporation (proliferation effects), gamma H2AX (senescence-associated DNA damage foci), autophagy using fluorescence microscopy on Operetta high content imager with unique machine learning algorithm for classification/quantification of senescent cells. SASP (senescence-associated secretory phenotype), molecular markers of senescence (CDKN2A [p16], CDKN1A [p21], TP53 [p53], LAMINB1), and disease-associated features (MMP9, MMP13, MMP3, ACAN) by qPCR and ELISA. Outcome: Validation of impact on FOXO3 isoform activity and subcellular localization, plus mitochondrial function, senescence kinetics, autophagy, cell survival in human primary cell types relevant to DDD in 2D.

content_blocks.title: Go/No-Go Point and Milestone 2 (Ex Vivo)
content_blocks.content: Go/No-Go Decision Criteria: Proceed to Milestone 2 based on successful achievement of Milestones 1a and 1b: specifically, identification of AONs that robustly shift FOXO3 isoform ratios and produce statistically significant improvement in at least two key markers of cellular health (e.g., reduced senescence, increased matrix production). Milestone 2 Objective: Evaluate ability of AONs targeting specific FOXO3 isoforms to modulate markers of disc health and degeneration in physiologically relevant ex vivo model of DDD. Workplan: Following assessment in monolayer, assess impact in ex vivo organotypic organ-on-a-chip cultures (and if available, primary human IVD explants). Ex vivo platforms incorporate human primary nucleus pulposus (NP) and annulus fibrosus (AF) cells induced into DDD-like phenotype using TNF-α and IL-1β (ref 27). Organ-on-a-chip configuration enables precise spatial arrangement of NP and AF cells replicating native disc architecture (NP cells in center, AF cells at periphery), plus co-culture with additional cell types (stromal fibroblasts). Allows evaluation of treatment effects on paracrine signaling of aged/damaged cells on unconnected cell populations. Disease-relevant outcomes: expression of collagens, fibromodulin, IL-6, IL-8, aggrecan (ACAN), MMP13, MMP9, MMP3, plus morphological analysis. Outcome: Validation data demonstrating impact of FOXO3-targeted AONs on molecular and structural features of disc degeneration in human-relevant ex vivo system, supporting further development for DDD.

content_blocks.title: Phase 1 Workplan Diagram
content_blocks.content: Diagram shows workflow from targeting sequences identified → MILESTONE 1a (Design AON) → two branches: (1) Evaluate in vitro (MILESTONE 1b) leading to target engagement readout; (2) Evaluate ex vivo (MILESTONE 2) leading to cell physiology readout. Visual includes icons for oligonucleotide design, cell culture plates, organ-on-chip device, and dose-response curve.

content_blocks.title: Go/No Go Milestones for Phase 2 and Phase 2 Plan
content_blocks.content: Phase 2 Go/No-Go Criteria: (1) Identification of appropriate prototype oligo producing desired effect on FOXO3 isoform usage and activity in 5nM to 400nM range. (2) Identification of appropriate prototype oligo with beneficial effects on cellular aging and disease markers in 2D monocultures and 3D human primary cell organoid models of DDD. Phase 2 Translation Round (subsequent fundraise): First steps would optimize oligo sequence and chemistries (routinely done by PI as CSO for SENISCA). Optimal oligo sequence deduced by 'walking' oligo base-by-base across sequence. Lead sequence then assessed as different nucleoside and backbone moieties at different dosages (typically 25nM to 400nM) in vitro for target engagement. In vivo PK/PD and Toxicology (CRO): Use appropriate rodent animal model for DDD (lumbar spine instability models, chemonucleosis, compression). PI has established CRO network through SENISCA. Outcome measures: macroscopic tests for pain/behavior, imaging, molecular features of inflammation/senescence/matrix remodeling. Standard toxicology assessment. CMC and toxicology packages (traditionally 2 mammalian species) undertaken later for AON drugs than conventional small molecules, as test item must match IND package exactly. PK/PD work may require oligo designed to mouse sequence (not human) if sequence not conserved, but this is conventional workflow for non-conserved oligos. Toxicology and CMC work begin once candidate molecule identified.

content_blocks.title: Phase 2 Workflow Diagram
content_blocks.content: Diagram shows progression from in vitro dataset (2025-2026) → ex vivo dataset → optimise sequence and chemistry → rodent PK/PD study → in vivo bioD and toxicology → second animal PK/PD study (NHP) → CMC → human phase 1 (extending to 2029+). Timeline shows 'This funding' covering 2025-2026 period, with 'Continuation funding' from initial filing through IND application. Includes illustrations of mouse, non-human primate, and human testing stages.

content_blocks.title: Path to Clinical for Oligonucleotide Geroprotective Drug
content_blocks.content: Figure 4 flowchart shows complete development pathway. Initial phases funded by initial investment cover: (1) Oligo design and validation; (2) In vitro dataset; (3) Ex vivo dataset; (4) In vivo dataset (rodent PK/PD study). Following phases: Oligo sequence and chemistry optimisation; Second animal PK/PD study (NHP) and toxicology; IND; CMC; Human phase 1. Timeline milestone: Initial filing occurs after in vivo dataset; IND application at end of process. Text explains: Some oligos conserved and can be assayed for efficacy in rodents; others may be primate-specific requiring non-human primates. Milestones and estimated timing to eventual IND approval are provided.

Reagents for Milestone 1a (Design and Validation of AONs): AON Oligos (8 sequences @ £200 each): £1,600 ($2,168); General Cellular/Molecular Consumables: £2,500 ($3,387); Oligofectamine: £1,600 ($2,168); Primary Cells (AF and NP): £2,200 ($2,981); Specialist Tissue Culture Reagents: £3,000 ($4,064); qPCR: £1,000 ($1,355); Laptop Computer for PDRA: £2,000 ($2,710). Reagents for Milestone 1b (Evaluation of AONs in vitro): Agilent Seahorse XF Cell Mito Stress Test Kit (6x96 well plates): £700 ($949); Antibodies: £1,250 ($1,694); Edu Kits: £500 ($678); H2Ax Kits: £600 ($813); Immunofluorescence Consumables: £750 ($1,016); Luciferase Reporter Gene Kits (2): £1,200 ($1,626); ELISA: £1,500 ($2,032); SAB Kits: £500 ($678); TUNEL: £900 ($1,219); Western Blotting: £500 ($678); qPCR: £1,000 ($1,355); Specialist Tissue Culture Reagents: £3,000 ($4,064). SUBTOTAL: £114,795 ($155,509). Go/No-Go Point noted at end of Year 1.

Reagents: OOC System (organ-on-chip): £7,806 ($10,570); Antibodies: £1,250 ($1,693); Immunofluorescence Consumables: £750 ($1,016); ELISA: £1,500 ($2,032); SAB Kits: £500 ($678); LDH: £250 ($339); Western Blotting: £500 ($678); Specialist Tissue Culture Reagents: £4,000 ($5,416). SUBTOTAL: £105,051 ($142,296). GRAND TOTAL: £219,846 or $297,805.

content_blocks.title: Financing and VitaDAO Funding Terms
content_blocks.content: Project financed through issuance of Intellectual Property Tokens (IPTs) on Bio Protocol's platform, anchored by $50,000 commitment from VitaDAO on same terms as all contributors, including aligned DAOs such as SpineDAO. Funding terms: Agreement for contract research services with Exeter University, granting Vitality Now (Swiss association representing VitaDAO) ownership to intellectual property developed by the project.

content_blocks.title: Team and Research Environment
content_blocks.content: Professor Lorna Harries: PhD from University College London 1994. Established RNA-mediated disease mechanisms group at Exeter 2006. Personal chair in Molecular Genetics at University of Exeter Medical School. Co-founder, co-director, Chief Scientific Officer at SENISCA Ltd (spin-out founded on Harries lab research). Awards: Diabetes UK RD Lawrence rising star 2011; Queen's Anniversary Prize for research excellence 2006 and 2019; Proteomass Lifetime Career Award 2021 for senescence and RNA processing. Publications: >160 peer-reviewed publications, >20,000 citations, H-index 56. Proven track record translating basic research; inventor on 2 patents with 7 more in draft. Research Team: PDRA exclusively focused on this project will benefit from wider Harries academic team of 7 individuals with multidisciplinary skills (primary cell biology, multi-omics, organ-on-chip systems, bioinformatics, molecular genetics). Complemented by co-location with Harries R&D team of SENISCA (16 people specializing in oligonucleotide drug development).

content_blocks.title: References (Selected Key Citations)
content_blocks.content: Key references include: Willcox et al. 2008 PNAS (FOXO3A genotype strongly associated with human longevity); Chang et al. 2023 Curr Mol Med (FOXO3 regulates aging and age-related diseases); Alvarez-Garcia et al. 2018 Aging Cell (FOXO required for intervertebral disk homeostasis, deficiency promotes degeneration); Frankum et al. 2021 Geroscience (extreme longevity variants at FOXO3 locus may moderate isoform levels); Wu et al. 2025 Drug Des Devel Ther (roles of FOXO3a in bone and cartilage diseases); Alvarez-Garcia et al. 2017 J Orthop Res (age-related reduction in FOXO expression correlates with IVD degeneration); Tan et al. 2023 Pharmaceuticals (FOXO3-activated circFGFBP1 inhibits ECM degradation and NP cell death in IVD degeneration); Min et al. 2025 Int Immunopharmacol (FOXO3a overexpression ameliorates IVD degeneration by decreasing NLRP3-mediated pyroptosis); Xu et al. 2019 J Immunol (novel role for FOXO3 Isoform2 in osteoclastic inhibition); Stein et al. 2010 Nucleic Acids Res (efficient gene silencing by locked nucleic acid ASOs without transfection reagents); Bennett 2019 Annu Rev Med (therapeutic ASOs coming of age).

content_blocks.title: Community Discussion - Response on Organ-on-Chip and NHP Models
content_blocks.content: Comment by bowtiedshrike (Aug 6, 2025): Organ-on-chip makes proposal stronger, especially in current US regulatory environment. May not need NHP as model if human biomarkers can be used instead. PI Response by Lorna_Harries (Aug 19, 2025): Move to human-relevant model systems with superior predictive power for adverse effects vs standard animal studies recently highlighted by FDA Modernisation Act 3.0, where FDA states animal work for IND submissions to be phased out over coming years. Some animal work necessary currently for basic toxicology/safety, but much can be done computationally, in human tissues ex vivo, and in organ-on-chip model systems. Animal models often not informative for PD outcomes. Use of relevant human ex vivo tissues and biomarkers often superior approach (adheres to this as centre lead for AFRUK Animal Free Centre of Excellence). Will monitor developments and work accordingly in development plan.

content_blocks.title: Community Discussion - Response on Safety and Biomarkers
content_blocks.content: Comment by Suhail (Aug 9, 2025): How will safety be tracked long-term given FOXO3's role in many body systems? Will there be early biomarkers to prove it's working before big trials? PI Response by Lorna_Harries (Aug 19, 2025): Safety is priority. Not targeting FOXO3 in entirety, but rather targeted, easily reversible, subtle manipulation of isoform balance, and only at site of action. Not inducing global supraphysiological FOXO3 expression (as some small molecules may), but looking to restore disordered isoform balance only in affected tissue. Oligonucleotide allows very precise targeting and, unlike gene therapy, only for period of time, so easy to discontinue if adverse impact. Local delivery provides protection against wider impacts. Local administration yields very little systemic exposure, so effects confined to injection site and environs, safeguarding against adverse effects on wider body. Long-term safety tracked by established markers of cardiac, hepatic, renal, respiratory health readily measured in clinic. Given FOXO3's role in metabolism, possible to monitor effects by assaying metabolic endpoints (glycolysis, gluconeogenesis, glycation markers). Toxicity issues with oligos mostly relate to nanoparticle carriers (not needed here), rare but related to inflammation, liver tox, complement activation. Defined sequence motifs predict this, screened for in oligo optimization along with predicted off-target. Before clinic (some way from that), asset screened in vivo and/or appropriate ex vivo models (DILI chips etc.). Many molecules examined for aging outcomes act on or intersect with upstream regulators of FOXO genes (Trametinib/MEK, AKT inhibitors, SIRT1 agonists, mTOR inhibitors), and their long-term safety profiles well understood. Biomarkers: No clinically recognized circulating biomarkers for DDD, but emerging area with promising candidates including GDF-15, IL-6, miR-155, miR-26a, ACAN, MMP2, MMP3, MMP7 (PMIDs 38156062, 34954738, 31770330). These markers have predictive/prognostic power for disease progression rate, readily measured in blood. Would be used with established clinical endpoints (spine MRI, CT, pain and mobility assessment).

content_blocks.title: Related Topics (Cross-References)
content_blocks.content: VDP-146 [Funding] The development of oligonucleotide drugs for healthspan: 19 replies, June 8, 2024. VDP-48 [Assessment]: Ikaria Therapeutics: 7 replies, June 26, 2022. VDP-29: Exosomes for treating systemic inflammation: 17 replies, July 25, 2022. VDP-18: Jonathan An- Towards reversing periodontal disease using Geroscience: 14 replies, March 29, 2022. VDP-16: Korolchuk Lab- Autophagy Activators Funding Proposal: 9 replies, January 15, 2022.

key_findings: FOXO3 isoform modulation is proposed as novel therapeutic approach for degenerative disc disease (DDD) and potentially other aging-related conditions
FOXO3 genetic variants (haplotype rs13217795-rs4946932-rs9400239) associated with ~36% increased chance of exceptional longevity across >11 human populations
FOXO3-long isoform (full-length, functional) vs FOXO3-short (lacks regulatory motifs, enriched in aging/degenerating tissues); aging tilts balance toward short form

Published evidence links FOXO3 to DDD: expression correlated with DDD in mouse and human, associated with ECM degradation and nucleus pulposus cell death, overexpression ameliorates IVD degeneration by decreasing NLRP3-mediated pyroptosis

Antisense oligonucleotide (AON) modality chosen for specificity, safety profile (acts on 20-25 nucleotide region only), local delivery capability (naked injection to intervertebral space), long duration of action (up to 6 months)
PI has ~30 years RNA biology experience, founder/CSO of SENISCA Ltd (oligonucleotide drug company), >160 peer-reviewed publications, H-index 56, 2 patents issued with 7 in draft

Phase 1 experimental plan: (1a) Design AONs using steric hindrance against isoform-specific translation start sites or negative regulatory elements; validate by Western blot and RT-qPCR in NHDF, HNPC, HAFC cells. (1b) Test functional effects on FOXO3 activity (luciferase reporter), subcellular localization (immunofluorescence), mitochondrial function (Seahorse XF24), senescence markers (SA-beta-Gal, EdU, gamma H2AX, autophagy via Operetta high content imaging with ML algorithm), SASP, molecular markers (p16, p21, p53, LAMINB1), disease markers (MMP9, MMP13, MMP3, ACAN) in 2D culture with DDD-like induction (TNFα, IL-1β, serum limitation). (2) Test in ex vivo organ-on-chip cultures with spatial arrangement of NP and AF cells mimicking native disc architecture, assess collagens, fibromodulin, IL-6, IL-8, ACAN, MMP13, MMP9, MMP3, morphology

Go/no-go criteria after Phase 1: AONs must robustly shift FOXO3 isoform ratios and produce statistically significant improvement in at least two key cellular health markers (e.g., reduced senescence, increased matrix production)

Phase 2 (future funding): Optimize oligo sequence/chemistry by base-by-base walking and testing different nucleoside/backbone moieties at 5-400nM range; conduct rodent PK/PD and toxicology in DDD animal models (lumbar spine instability, chemonucleosis, compression); conduct CMC; path to IND

Market opportunity: DDD market $35B global (2025) projected to $45B (2029) with 7.5% CAGR; competitive landscape less crowded than cancer/CVD/diabetes; competitors are non-disease modifying (hydrogels), non-specific pathway modulators (TGFβ), or biologicals (stem cells)
Future label expansion potential: Cardiovascular disease ($160B), cancer ($200B), autoimmune/immune dysfunction ($170B), diabetes ($40B), neurodegenerative disorders ($20B), sarcopenia/frailty ($4B)

IP strategy: Phase 1 data sufficient for initial patent filing (prototype oligos + demonstration of senescence/disease marker attenuation in 2D/3D culture); claims bolstered by in vivo data within 12 months (Phase 2 funding)

Funding structure: IPT issuance on Bio Protocol platform; $50k anchor from VitaDAO; SpineDAO expression of interest; contract research agreement with Exeter University; IP ownership to Vitality Now (VitaDAO Swiss association)

Budget £219,846 ($297,805) covers: PDRA salary (£70,124 x 2 years), PI time (2hr/week, £18,371 x 2 years), reagents for AON synthesis (8 sequences), primary cells (HNPC, HAFC), tissue culture reagents, assay kits (Seahorse, immunofluorescence, luciferase reporter, ELISA, Western blot, qPCR), organ-on-chip system (£7,806)

Safety considerations addressed: Local delivery limits systemic exposure; oligonucleotides are reversible (not permanent like gene therapy); defined sequence motifs screened to avoid inflammation/liver tox/complement activation; clinical safety monitoring via cardiac/hepatic/renal/respiratory markers and metabolic endpoints; many upstream FOXO regulators already have well-characterized safety profiles (MEK, AKT, SIRT1, mTOR inhibitors)

Emerging biomarkers for DDD: GDF-15, IL-6, miR-155, miR-26a, ACAN, MMP2, MMP3, MMP7 have predictive/prognostic power for disease progression; can be combined with established clinical endpoints (spine MRI, CT, pain/mobility assessment)
